text.title: Mistake Capture Rules
text.summary: When to capture a mistake, minimum required fields, and quality standards for entries.

rule.statement: Document a mistake within 24 hours of the failure. Do not defer — details fade within a single conversation turn.
rule.rationale: Incomplete entries written days later omit root cause and specific context, making them useless for future warning injection.

rule.statement: Capture when: a bug reaches production, CI fails unexpectedly, the same error occurs twice, a task takes more than 2x estimated tokens, or an agent hallucinates an API or feature.
rule.rationale: These signals identify pattern-level failures worth documenting. One-off typos do not require entries.

rule.statement: Every MIS-NNN entry must include: severity (critical/high/medium/low), what happened (specific description), root cause (Five Whys chain, minimum 3 levels), and one concrete prevention step.
rule.rationale: Entries without root cause cannot generate useful prevention rules. Entries without prevention are read-only history, not actionable warnings.

antipattern.description: Prevention step is "be more careful" or "review before committing".
antipattern.reason: These are not actionable. A concrete prevention step specifies a trigger and an action: "When task keywords include 'migration', run COUNT(*) on affected tables before planning."

antipattern.description: Root cause attributed to "insufficient context" without identifying the specific missing piece.
antipattern.reason: Every LLM failure can be attributed to "insufficient context" — this is always true and always useless. Identify what specific documentation, example, or constraint was absent.

antipattern.description: Entry title does not include domain keywords: "MIS-007: Bug in code".
antipattern.reason: Keyword search is the primary retrieval mechanism. Domain keywords in the title ("MIS-007: Database Migration — Missing Row Count Check") enable reliable pre-task matching.

rule.statement: When a pattern recurs a third time, add an automated CI prevention rule; documenting the same entry twice is not sufficient.
rule.rationale: Documentation is awareness, not enforcement. Recurring patterns require automated blocking to actually prevent recurrence.

example.code:
## MIS-007: Database Migration — Missing Row Count Check
**Severity:** High **What happened:** Migration timed out after 30 min on production (2.8M rows). **Root cause:** 1. Why did migration time out? Transaction locked the entire table. 2. Why was it a single transaction? ALTER TABLE applied to all rows at once. 3. Why not batched? Row count not checked before choosing migration strategy. **Prevention:** Before any migration, run `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM <table>`. If >100k rows, use batched approach (PAT-002).
example.note: Three levels of why, specific numbers, concrete prevention with a reference to a known pattern.

text.reference: MIS-NNN entry template with all required fields
